title: The Founder of a $1.5B AI Company on What Comes After the First Wave of AI Apps

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: You are the cofounder and CEO of Granolah.
One of the first new AI apps where people were like, holy shit.
This actually works.

paragraphs.zh: 你是 Granolah 的 cofounder 和 CEO。这是最早一批让人直呼“holy shit”的新 AI app 之一。它居然真的能用。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Meeting notes are not the end all, be all value that everyone's running after.
There's something much bigger.
I think we are in the very, very early steps of a computing revolution.
What has come thus far will pale in comparison to what will come soon.
Startups are like knife fights.

paragraphs.zh: 会议纪要并不是所有人都在追逐的那种终极价值。还有更大的东西。我觉得，我们正处在一场计算革命极其、极其早期的阶段。迄今为止出现的一切，和很快将要到来的相比，都会显得逊色。创业公司就像持刀肉搏。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And I thought startups were just really hard when they weren't working.
Turns out they're really hard even when they're working as well.

paragraphs.zh: 我以前以为，startup 只有在不顺的时候才会特别难。后来才发现，就算一切进展顺利，它也一样非常难。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Every is the only subscription you need to stay at the edge of AI.
If you care about being on top of the latest models and using the latest tools, you have to subscribe to Every.
We'll help you separate the signal from the noise.
Go to every.to/ subscribe today.
And now back to the episode.

paragraphs.zh: Every 是你保持在 AI 前沿所需的唯一订阅。如果你在乎紧跟最新 model、使用最新工具，那你就必须订阅 Every。我们会帮你从噪音中分离出真正有价值的信号。现在就去 every.to/ 订阅。接下来回到本期节目。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Chris, welcome to the show.

paragraphs.zh: Chris，欢迎来到节目。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Hey, Dan.
Great to see you.

paragraphs.zh: 嘿，Dan。很高兴见到你。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Good to see too.
It's been a while.
So for people who don't know, you are the cofounder and CEO of Granolah, one of my favorite AI apps.
One of the apps that I think really kicked off the wave in the in, like, you know, I guess, two two or three years ago.
It was the one of the first, like, new AI apps where people were like, holy shit.

paragraphs.zh: 也很高兴见到你。已经有一阵子了。给不了解情况的人介绍一下：你是 Granolah 的 cofounder 和 CEO，也是我最喜欢的 AI app 之一的创始人。它是我认为真正开启了这波浪潮的 app 之一，大概就是两三年前吧。它是最早一批全新的 AI app 之一，当时人们看到都会说，holy shit。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: This actually works, and it's useful.
And since then, you've gone on to I I think the team is about 55.
You've you've you've raised at, a $1,500,000,000 valuation.
You're just killing it.
And we had a really good conversation in December 2024, so almost two and a half years ago about putting soul into your product and making products that are delightful.

paragraphs.zh: 这东西居然真的能用，而且很实用。从那以后，你们一路发展壮大——我想团队现在大概有 55 人了。你们融资时的估值达到了 $1,500,000,000。你们的发展势头非常猛。我们在 2024 年 12 月有过一次非常好的对谈，聊的是如何把灵魂注入你的产品，以及如何打造让人愉悦的产品；那已经是将近两年半前了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And I'm just super curious to hear what's on your mind, how things are going, and to sort of share out both of our journeys over the last couple of years and and think about the future together.

paragraphs.zh: 我就是特别想听听你最近在想什么、事情进展得怎么样，也想把我们俩过去这几年的经历拿出来一起聊聊，并且一起想想未来。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Couldn't be more excited to chat.
I I can I've been following all the different things you've been doing, from a distance.
I'd to hear the latest from the inside on how that's going.
Just to react on one thing you said, you said we're killing it, right?
And we're very lucky, things are going well.

paragraphs.zh: 我非常期待这次聊天。我一直有点隔着距离关注你在做的各种事情。很想听听你从内部视角讲讲最新进展到底怎么样。顺便回应一下你刚才说的一点，你说我们现在做得很猛，对吧？我们确实很幸运，事情进展得不错。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Something I was unprepared for, so I did a previous startup and startups are, they're knife fights, right?
They're really, I don't know, maybe life's easy for you, but for me it's really hard.
And I thought startups were just really hard when they weren't working.
Turns out they're really hard when they're working as well.
It's just, it's a knife fight when things are going well or not going well, and I was a bit unprepared for that.

paragraphs.zh: 有一件事是我之前完全没准备到的。我以前做过一家 startup（创业公司），而 startup 就像持刀肉搏，对吧？真的非常难。我不知道，也许你的人生比较轻松，但对我来说这事真的很难。我原本以为 startup 只有在不顺的时候才特别难。结果发现，它在顺的时候也一样很难。就是说，不管事情进展顺利还是不顺利，都是一场持刀肉搏，而我之前对此有点准备不足。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So it's just, I'm very, I had a professor in journalism school who, he was basically like the last thing the world needs is another profile that puts someone up, you know, it's like oh this person's so great.
And it's like no, no, Everything's hard today.
If I fight day in, day out, like that's that's my mentality.
Just wanted to share that.

paragraphs.zh: 所以就是，我很——我以前在新闻学院有位教授，他基本上的意思是，这个世界最不需要的，就是再来一篇把某个人高高捧起来的人物特写，好像在说“哦，这个人真是太厉害了”。但其实不是，不是。如今一切都很难。我每天都在硬扛、日复一日地拼，这就是我的心态。只是想分享一下这一点。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I feel you, man.

I definitely feel the same way where I've been doing companies for a long time and Every is the first one that's, like, really growing and just really working in this way that feels like it's a real company.
We have 30 people now, you know?
Congrats.
Thank you.

paragraphs.zh: 我懂你，兄弟。我绝对也有同感。我做公司已经很多年了，而 Every 是第一家真正开始增长、真正以这种方式运转起来、让人感觉它是一家真正公司的公司。我们现在有 30 个人了，你知道吗？恭喜。谢谢。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And when it is working yeah.
Like, a good example, clogged tag dropped yesterday.
We've been working on a Slack agent.
Clogged tag dropped yesterday.
And so then everyone's looking at you like, what do we do?

paragraphs.zh: 而当它真的开始运转时，没错——举个很好的例子，clogged tag 昨天发布了。我们一直在做一个 Slack agent（智能体）。clogged tag 昨天发布了。然后所有人都会看着你，问：我们现在该怎么办？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And I'm like, we've we've we're prepared for this.
We've been thinking we know that we knew this was gonna happen.
And the especially in AI, I feel like the ground shifts so quickly.

And I I assume for you, like, one of the things you're you're talking to, and I'm talking about it, and I'm actually really curious, is you were the first one to do really great AI meeting notes and meeting transcriptions.
And then immediately, everyone just, like, put meeting notes into their product.

paragraphs.zh: 而我会说，我们——我们其实早就为这件事做了准备。我们一直在思考，我们知道这事会发生。尤其是在 AI 领域，我感觉地面变化得特别快。我想对你来说也是这样——有一件你正在谈、我也一直在谈，而且我真的很好奇的事是：你们是第一批把 AI meeting notes（会议纪要）和 meeting transcriptions（会议转录）做得特别出色的人。结果紧接着，所有人都像是一下子把 meeting notes 塞进了自己的产品里。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: We actually we have a we have this product called Monologue that has me a meeting notes button, you know?

paragraphs.zh: 其实我们也有一个叫 Monologue 的产品，它里面就有一个 meeting notes 按钮，你知道的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Oh, cool.

paragraphs.zh: 哦，挺酷的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I didn't know that.
So we're, like, slightly competitive.
Yeah.
I don't I don't really think of it as being an exact an exact one to one replacement.
I actually have both on my computer, but but still, that's probably what you're referring to when you say knife fight.

paragraphs.zh: 我之前不知道这个。所以我们算是有点竞争关系。对。不过我不太会把它看成一种完全一比一的替代品。其实我电脑里两个都有，但你说的 knife fight，大概指的就是这个。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So tell me about that.
What is that like?
How has that actually affected your business at all?

paragraphs.zh: 那你说说这个吧。那是一种什么感觉？它实际上到底对你的业务产生了什么影响吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: That's actually not what I was referring to as a knife fight.
I mean, that's part of it.
I think it's just, I guess what I was referring to is, I think by definition, a startup is, you're either fighting for survival.

You're always fighting for survival and it's like, if things are going well, you're fighting to, it's like this big wave and you're like on the surfboard and you're trying desperately not to fall off that surfboard.
And so you're kind of always just beyond the edge of your abilities.

paragraphs.zh: 其实我说的 knife fight 不是单指那个。我是说，那只是其中一部分。我想我真正想表达的是，我觉得按定义来说，startup 就是在为生存而战。你总是在为生存而战；如果事情进展顺利，那你也是在拼命跟上，就像有一道巨浪，你站在冲浪板上，拼命不让自己从板上掉下来。所以你基本上总是在自己能力边界之外一点点的地方。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And it's almost like by definition and therefore I'm always doing, you always hear this as like, oh, it's like as a founder, you're always doing stuff you don't know how to do.
It's like, oh yeah, actually that at least that had been my experience.
It's like, I'm always just in the space where like it's stuff I've never done before or it's just more than we've ever like bit off in the past.
Yeah, the competition, like here's my view and I feel like I've said this, let me think about it.
When I think about this is going sound like one of those bullshit answers around competition.

paragraphs.zh: 而且几乎可以说这是定义使然，所以我一直都在做——你总会听人这么说：哦，作为 founder，你总是在做自己不会做的事。感觉就是，哦，对，至少我的经历确实是这样。我好像一直都处在那种区域里：要么是我以前从没做过的事，要么就是我们以前从没吃下过这么大的挑战。对，至于竞争，我的看法是这样，而且我感觉我以前也说过这个，让我想想。我现在这么说，听起来可能会像那种关于竞争的 bullshit 式回答。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: There's always the interview where it's like, Hey, Microsoft just copied your product.
How do you feel about it?
And the founder has to come up with some answer.
The way I view it is I think we are in the very, very early steps of a computing revolution and that there's What has come thus far will pale in comparison to what will come soon.
I think that it doesn't Meeting notes are not the end all, be all value that everyone's running after.

paragraphs.zh: 总会有那种采访：嘿，Microsoft 刚刚抄了你的产品，你对此什么感受？然后 founder 就得想办法给出一个回答。我的看法是，我认为我们正处在一场计算革命的非常非常早期阶段，而到目前为止出现的一切，和接下来很快会到来的东西相比，都会显得黯然失色。我觉得，meeting notes 并不是所有人追逐的终极价值。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: There's something much bigger, and I think it's what interface we use for work and how we work and what does that look like in the AI native world.
When I think about competition, I'm just kind of like, what people are fighting for today doesn't matter.
There's this incredible opportunity ahead.
And I think we have a shot at it and a few other companies have a shot at it, right?
That's really what matters.

paragraphs.zh: 真正更大的东西在别处，我觉得那就是：我们在工作中使用什么样的 interface（界面），我们如何工作，以及在 AI native 世界里这些会是什么样子。当我想到竞争时，我基本会觉得，人们今天争夺的东西并不重要。前面有一个极其巨大的机会。我觉得我们有机会抓住它，也有几家公司同样有机会，对吧？真正重要的是这个。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So in the same way where people are like, Oh, Granolah's doing really well.
It's like, well, in my mind it's like easy come, easy go in a way, right?
Like meeting notes are useful, but like a lot of things are going to change in the future.
Just because people use us today doesn't mean they're going use us for that in the future if we're not the best at that next thing.
So yeah, don't know, that's my zoomed out view.

paragraphs.zh: 所以同样地，当人们说，哦，Granolah 现在做得很好。我心里会觉得，某种程度上这也是 easy come, easy go，对吧？比如 meeting notes 确实有用，但未来很多事情都会改变。人们今天使用我们，并不意味着如果我们在下一件事上做不到最好，他们未来还会继续因为这个而使用我们。所以，是啊，我也不知道，这就是我更宏观的看法。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And the other thing to say is we were not the first meeting notetaker, right?
Meaning like AI notetakers have been around for ages.
We were, let's say, heavily inspired by a lot of things that came before.
So I can't be like, Oh, how dare you be inspired by what we did?
It's just in an ecosystem.

paragraphs.zh: 还有一点要说的是，我们并不是第一个做会议记录的 notetaker，对吧？也就是说，AI notetaker 这种东西早就存在很多年了。可以说，我们在很大程度上受到了很多前人做法的启发。所以我也不能说，哦，你怎么敢受我们启发？这本来就是同一个生态系统里的事情。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: It has affected the company a lot less than I thought it would.
I think within Notion copied or whatever, let's say launch something, I think heavily inspired by granola, OpenAI did, Zoom did recently.
So it's like in some ways it's like I didn't have to sit with the nightmare submit scenario of like, what would it be like the day that launches?
It's like that kind of came and happened and we're

paragraphs.zh: 它对公司的影响，比我原本以为的小得多。我觉得 Notion 算是 copy 了，或者说，姑且算是发布了某种东西，我认为那明显受了 granola 的启发，OpenAI 做了，Zoom 最近也做了。所以某种意义上，我不必一直坐在那里幻想那种噩梦场景：等那一天他们发布时会怎么样？因为那件事某种程度上已经发生了，而我们——

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: It's clear.
Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 这很清楚。对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yeah, we're still like it hasn't changed anything from our growth rate or whatnot.
But again, we're still a drop in the bucket compared to what we'd like to be in terms of how people use us and how many people use us.
So I think it's just very early days is my view.

paragraphs.zh: 对，我们现在依然是——它并没有改变我们的增长率之类的东西。但再说一次，和我们希望达到的规模相比，不管是人们如何使用我们，还是有多少人在使用我们，我们现在都还只是沧海一粟。所以我的看法是，现在仍然非常早期。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: That makes sense.
I just got to say I love getting to talk to you because I feel like you're just so honest about what you're dealing with.
It's it's the best.
It's so fun.
It's so it's very different.

paragraphs.zh: 这很有道理。我得说一句，我很喜欢和你聊天，因为我觉得你对自己正在面对的事情真的非常坦诚。这种感觉太好了。特别有意思。也真的很不一样。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: It's very different from a lot of a lot of people, and I think this is gonna be a very fun conversation.
But one of the but what what I wanna go back to is and I'm happy to share too.
What I want to go back to is you said you're balancing on the surfboard and you're trying not to fall off.
And, you know, normally in startups, you're like, you're on the surfboard and you're just, like, paddling and you're, like, waiting for the wave.
And then I think for both of us for you more so than me, but for both of us, you're like, I'm on the big one now.

paragraphs.zh: 这和很多很多人都很不一样，我觉得这会是一场非常有意思的对话。不过我想回到一点上——当然我也愿意分享——我想回到的是，你刚才说你像是在冲浪板上努力保持平衡，尽量别掉下去。你知道，通常在 startup 里，更像是你站在冲浪板上，不停划水，等着浪来。然后我觉得对我们俩来说——对你尤其如此，比我更明显——现在更像是，你会说，我现在已经上到那个大浪上了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Like, let's try not to get let's try not to fall off and just get pounded, you know?
And so what what was what are some of those things recently where you're you've felt like you've been at the edge of your ability and you're like, I gotta figure this.
I gotta figure this out.
This is this is maybe it's a good thing, but generally it's like, shit.
This is very different.

paragraphs.zh: 就像，尽量别被甩下去，别被浪狠狠干翻，你懂吧？所以，最近有哪些事情让你觉得自己已经到了能力边缘，会想“我得把这事搞明白，我得把这事搞定”？也许这总体上是件好事，但通常第一反应还是，靠，这太不一样了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: So a lot of it's kind of my limitation to lack of experience as a founder.
So like my last startup, Socratic, we were 12 people.
Just going from 12 to We're actually over We're somewhere between, I don't know, sixty and seventy now.
That's very different, and I've never done that before.
Organizing humans, that's one.

paragraphs.zh: 很大一部分其实来自于我作为 founder 经验不足这一局限。所以比如我上一个 startup，Socratic，我们当时只有 12 个人。光是从 12 个人增长到——我们现在其实已经超过——大概在六七十人之间吧。这就非常不一样了，而我以前从没经历过。组织和管理人，这是一方面。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Something that's that's challenging and I I wish I had I wish I had, like, a really succinct, beautiful answer.
Like, we have it all figured out, but it's in an era where you like, you're talking about product having soul and product feeling consistent and coherent and tasteful.
And how do you scale that?

Like, when you have more people working on that, how do you how do you how can you both get the upside of having lots of people working on these hard problems, but also having this consistent feeling?
That's a really hard problem.

paragraphs.zh: 这件事很有挑战，我真希望自己有一个特别简洁、漂亮的答案。好像我们已经把一切都想明白了那样。但现在这个时代，你谈的是 product 要有灵魂，要让 product 的感觉保持一致、连贯、有品味。而这要怎么规模化？当有更多人一起参与时，你怎么才能既获得很多人共同攻克这些难题的好处，又同时保住这种一致的感觉？这真的是个很难的问题。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I think, actually had, well, there's so many different interesting topics for us to dive into.
But one is like the traditional roles of PM and design and engineering and how you group those people together and what are their responsibilities.
It feels like what has worked in the past may not make sense exactly one to one in the world of the future, but I don't actually know how to define roles and split work.
There's something really valuable if you're like, Oh, you're the designer and the engineer, or vice versa.
Okay, now we know where we collaborate, where the interface line is.

paragraphs.zh: 我觉得，其实这里面有很多很有意思的话题可以展开。但其中一个就是传统的 PM、design 和 engineering 这些角色，以及你怎么把这些人组织在一起、他们各自该负责什么。感觉过去行之有效的方式，在未来的世界里未必还能一比一照搬；但说实话，我现在也不知道该怎么定义角色、怎么切分工作。如果你能明确说“哦，你是 designer，他是 engineer”，或者反过来，那其实有很大的价值。这样大家就知道该在哪里协作，接口边界在哪里。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Do you guys have a philosophy on that at every year?

paragraphs.zh: 你们在 Every 每年都会形成一套关于这个的哲学吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I don't know.
We've done different things and it sort of depends on the situation and the product.
My one mental model that I have that seems to work especially for early products is there are two roles that matter.
One is called the pirate and one's called the architect, And the pirate's job is to just build as fast as possible to find something valuable.
And it can be vibe coded.

paragraphs.zh: 我不知道。我们尝试过不同做法，而且某种程度上要看具体情况和具体 product。我的一个 mental model（心智模型）是：尤其对于早期 product，真正重要的只有两种角色。一个叫 pirate，一个叫 architect。pirate 的工作就是尽可能快地去 build，去找到有价值的东西。而且它甚至可以是 vibe coded。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: You don't have to really think about the architecture, all that kind of stuff.

And the architect's job is to pair with the pirate and think about, as the pirate finds something that's valuable, how do we make this into a system that is sustainable, that we understand, that can scale?
And what's interesting is I think you need one pirate per product.
But you can have one architect work on multiple products.

You can have them bounce around because the the tools now are so good that someone who's a really good programmer, really good architect can just drop into a code base and be like, I'm going to learn this code base in in an hour because I just have Claude or Codex or whatever map it for me, and then I'll identify the structural pillars that the invariants that need to be true in this product for it to work well.

Here's the new plan.

paragraphs.zh: 而且还会冒出这些小问题，比如，好，您什么时候搬出、什么时候搬入？Codex 都知道，因为我之前一直在跟它聊我什么时候搬家、什么时候想切换。然后它就会说，你看，你原来的 plan 不能用了，这是新的 plan。

And I don't have to do any of that.
I'm just sitting there.

paragraphs.zh: 它知道——而且会像是——我不想要任何 hidden fees。它知道该怎么计算，你知道，这是不是一笔划算的交易。它还能去做 research，然后推动对方把它认为我应该拿到的东西给我。而这些我都不用自己做。我就只是坐在那里。

sentences.text: And is that something that you somehow communicated to Codex or is that just an out of the box?

It's easy because it's easy for it.

paragraphs.zh: 两者都有。它之所以容易，是因为这对它来说很容易。它相当清楚什么时候可以做 assumptions（假设），什么时候不该做。所以我既可以在 loop（回路）里参与它，也可以切出去，到另一个 thread（线程）里做别的事。所以我有点像是在和它来回切换。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I think that's something.

Built called Tend.

paragraphs.zh: 我觉得那是一回事。但还有一个更普遍的点，就是我现在处理 email 也是这么做的。它会和我一起在 Quora 里 loop，我们的 email app 也是这样。另外，我还有一个自己实验着做出来的 open source 东西，叫 Tend。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: But basically, it's like my emails are now cards that I read in Codex in the in app browser.

Do you want to send it?

paragraphs.zh: 基本上，我的 emails 现在变成了一张张卡片，我在 Codex 的 app browser 里阅读。然后每张卡片都会说：这封邮件讲了什么。这是我认为合适的 draft（草稿）。你要发送吗？然后我就直接和它对话。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I see.

paragraphs.zh: 我明白了。所以基本上，你这是 vibe coded（凭感觉编码）了一个你自己的 email client，对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Is that I vibe coded an experimental email client that does this, and then we will have a version of Quora, which is our full email client that just has us out of the box.

But you can think of the apps that are structured this way as being I've been calling them Codex native apps, but they're basically apps that are responsible for saving the state and rendering the UI, and you bring your agent to it.
And I think that that is a much it's a very powerful paradigm because think about I mean, think about all the work that you have to do for granola to make an agent that is good.
And it's really hard.
And you're also competing with the clods and the codexes of the world who are also building the same kind of agent with the same kind of functionality.

paragraphs.zh: 是的，我 vibe coded 了一个会做这些事的实验性 email client，然后我们会推出一个版本的 Quora，也就是我们完整的 email client，它开箱即用 out of the box 就带这些功能。但你可以把这类以这种方式构建的 app 理解为——我一直称它们为 Codex native apps——本质上，这类 app 负责保存 state（状态）并渲染 UI，而你把自己的 agent 带进去。我认为这是一种非常强大的 paradigm（范式），因为你想想看，为了让 granola 里有一个足够好的 agent，你得做多少工作。这真的很难。而且你还在和 clods、codexes 这类玩家竞争，他们也在构建同一种 agent，具备类似的功能。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And to some degree, I also think that in some cases, that's very worthwhile because I do think two agents is better than one.
But ultimately, I really want to be able to, for example, bring and I think you're probably seeing this with a lot of granola users.
I really want to bring my agent to granola.
You already know how AI is changing how everyday work gets done, how much ground you can cover, and how fast a team can scale.
To stay ahead, you need tools that give you a competitive advantage built for this new era.

paragraphs.zh: 在某种程度上，我也认为在一些情况下这非常值得，因为我确实觉得两个 agents 比一个更好。但归根结底，比如说，我真正想要的是把我的 agent 带到 granola 里。我觉得你可能已经在很多 granola 用户身上看到了这一点：我真的很想把我的 agent 带到 granola。你已经知道 AI 正在如何改变日常工作的完成方式、你能覆盖多少工作量，以及一个团队能以多快的速度扩张。要保持领先，你需要的是为这个新时代打造、能给你带来竞争优势的工具。

sentences.text: It's extensible with an API and MCP access, and is built with infrastructure to keep up with your most ambitious agents.
It's loved by high growth startups like Granola, Modal, WhisperFlow, and Every.

Go to adio.com/every and get 15% off your first year.

paragraphs.zh: 它可通过 API 和 MCP 访问进行扩展，并且底层基础设施的设计足以跟上你最有野心的 agents。包括 Granola、Modal、WhisperFlow 和 Every 在内的高增长初创公司都很喜欢它。Everywin 背后的工作就是由 Adio 驱动的。这就是 Adio，agentic CRM。访问 adio.com/every，首年可享 15% 折扣。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: That's adio.com/every.
And now back to the episode.

paragraphs.zh: 就是 adio.com/every。现在回到本期节目。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: What do you think like, yeah.
You you keep saying that, and I'm really excited about that idea.
I wonder how does that work in practice?
Forget Granolah, let's say you, I don't know, can you do this with Quora right now?
Or how could we build an app that would allow you to bring your own agent that's not MCP?

paragraphs.zh: 你一直在说这个，我对这个想法也真的很兴奋。我想知道它在实际中到底是怎么运作的？先别说 Granolah，假设拿 Quora 来说——我不确定，你现在能用 Quora 做这个吗？或者说，我们要怎么构建一个 app，让你可以接入你自己的 agent，而且它不是通过 MCP 的？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Because it feels a little bit different, right?
Because it's like, you still want to have the UI.
That's the difference.
It's like you and the agent are both looking at the same UI and manipulating the same thing.

paragraphs.zh: 因为这感觉还是有点不一样，对吧？因为你仍然希望保留 UI。这就是差别所在。也就是说，你和 agent 都在看同一个 UI，也都在操作同一个东西。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: MCP is part of it.
But yes, I think MCP is Historically, you're thinking about it as the agent is going to interact with the app and I'm not going to interact with the app.
And I think what this paradigm says is actually you want your agent and the user to be interacting with the app at the same time and be able to trade off.
And so the way that you do that is, one, yeah, you give the agent access to an MCP or a CLI.
And then you give the user access to a web interface.

paragraphs.zh: MCP 是其中的一部分。但没错，我觉得 MCP——从历史上看，你会把它理解成：agent 会去和 app 交互，而我不会去和 app 交互。而我认为，这种范式真正表达的是：其实你希望 agent 和用户同时与这个 app 交互，而且两者之间可以相互切换、分工协作。所以实现方式是，第一，没错，你给 agent 提供 MCP 或者 CLI 的访问；然后你再给用户提供一个 web interface。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And the user is using the web interface inside of an in app browser of a codex or of a Cloud Code.
And then the agent can decide, do I want to help the user or collaborate with the user via using the browser use or via using the CLI, which I think CLI is somewhat better.
But the trick is the CLI has to then modify the state of the UI.

paragraphs.zh: 用户是在 codex 或 Cloud Code 的 app 内浏览器里使用这个 web interface。然后 agent 可以决定：我是要通过使用浏览器来帮助用户或与用户协作，还是通过 CLI 来协作？我觉得 CLI 某种程度上更好一些。但关键在于，CLI 接着必须能够修改 UI 的状态。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Right, exactly.
That's the thing.
It's like that's the thing that needs to be.

So it's almost like if you had hypothetically, let's say you could have an MCP, right, that actually had a stateful, like the MCP could modify the state, real time state of a UI that was rendered for a user.
Ideally there was at least a one to one mapping of anything you can do in the UI could also be done through the MCP.

paragraphs.zh: 对，完全没错。关键就在这里。这就是那个必须被解决的点。所以几乎可以这样想：假设你有一个 MCP，对吧，而且这个 MCP 是有状态的——也就是说，MCP 可以修改一个为用户渲染出来的 UI 的实时状态。理想情况下，至少应该做到一一对应：任何你能在 UI 里完成的事，也都应该能通过 MCP 来完成。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: You have an agent and a human interface.
Because computer use or like it's like the web browser use feels like a it's unnecessarily slow.
Right?
It's like a it's a hack and not not.
Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 你有一个 agent 和一个人类界面。因为 computer use，或者说像用 web browser 那样的使用方式，感觉有点不必要地慢。对吧？这有点像是一种 hack，而不是，不是……对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: That's really interesting.
Yeah, there's something about the presence of the agent.
This is a fascinating topic.
I haven't thought about it very much, but it's the embodiment of the agent is also maybe interesting.
It's like when you're in Figma, can see other people's locations on the There's something there that you maybe would lose if there was just an MCP and the human Maybe not.

paragraphs.zh: 这真的很有意思。对，agent 的“存在感”这件事里确实有点东西。这是个很迷人的话题。我之前没怎么认真想过，但 agent 的 embodiment（具身化、呈现方式）可能也很有意思。就像你在 Figma 里的时候，可以看到其他人在……上的位置。这里面有某种东西，如果只有一个 MCP 和人类，也许你就会失去。也可能不会。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I don't know how you Does the agent just feel like it's just the computer and that's all the same?
Or does it feel like it's an entity that you're both looking at the same thing together?

paragraphs.zh: 我不知道你是怎么……agent 会不会只是让人感觉它不过就是那台 computer，其他都一样？还是说，它会让人感觉像是一个实体，而你们两个正在一起看同样的东西？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: It's more that.
It's in the same way that with the Google Docs.
We have this markdown editor called Proof that we built.
And in that one, can see Codex has entered the document and you can see where it is.

paragraphs.zh: 更像是后者。这有点像在 Google Docs 里那样。我们自己做了一个 markdown editor，叫 Proof。在那个里面，你可以看到 Codex 已经进入了文档，也能看到它所在的位置。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Oh, that's cool.
How do you do that?
How does Codex enter the document?

paragraphs.zh: 哦，这很酷。你们是怎么做到的？Codex 是怎么进入文档的？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: It's just the same kind of CLI MCP type situation, but it has a command that's like, Okay, set presence, set location in the document, that kind of thing.
And agents are very good at knowing how to do that.
But there's so many interesting, then, UI challenges to figure out because an agent can do like a thousand different actions at once.
And so how do you show that to a user?

paragraphs.zh: 本质上还是同一种 CLI + MCP 类型的方案，只不过它有一个命令，类似“好，设置 presence（存在状态）”“设置在文档里的位置”这类东西。而 agent 很擅长知道该怎么做这些事。不过接下来就有很多很有意思的 UI 挑战要解决，因为一个 agent 可以同时做上千种不同的动作。那你要怎么把这些展示给用户看呢？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: It's interesting.
That's that's actually not where my brain went.
Because like, you're right.
It's like, yeah, it's like, if if you wanna see what the agent's doing, that's one problem.
I just thinking in like an email, it's like it'd be nice for it to know where your mouse is.

paragraphs.zh: 很有意思。那其实不是我一开始想到的方向。因为你说得对。就是说，如果你想看到 agent 在做什么，那是一个问题。我刚刚想到的是，比如在 email 里，如果它能知道你的鼠标在哪儿，会很好。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: It'd be nice to be able to like, know, be like, oh, this part of the email is not great.
And just have that be, you know, either you highlight the text or whatnot.
And that's not really possible today unless you explicitly Like how would you have to do that?
It would have to be like looking at pixels on your screen, right?
There's no native way to do that unless you build it into a UI and you're like passing that information to the agent, right?

paragraphs.zh: 如果它能像这样知道，比如说，“哦，这一部分 email 写得不太好”，那会很不错。然后你就可以，比如高亮那段文字之类的。但在今天这其实做不到，除非你显式地……你到底得怎么做呢？它必须得去看你屏幕上的 pixels（像素），对吧？除了把这个能力直接做进某个 UI 里，然后把这些信息传给 agent，否则并没有原生的办法做到，对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: More or less, I think the way that I do it is each so each piece of information that I want to do, want to take care of is represented in a card.
And then I can talk to the card.
I can say like, yeah, it doesn't it doesn't necessarily know where my mouse is, but it knows that I'm talking to this part of the UI.
And then

paragraphs.zh: 大体上，我觉得我的做法是，把我想处理、想照看的每一条信息都表示成一张 card。然后我就可以和这张 card 交互。我可以说，没错，它不一定知道我的鼠标在哪儿，但它知道我正在和 UI 的这一部分说话。然后——

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And it just knows because you say that when you're talking to it or because that is the the main thing that's foregrounded in the app?

paragraphs.zh: 所以它知道，只是因为你在和它说话时这么指出来了，还是因为那是 app 里当前处在前景、最被突出显示的那个东西？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Whatever I'm looking at is currently focused.
And so and I know that it's focused.
And then when I'm talking to, it just knows to put to put that context next to that thing that I'm looking at.

paragraphs.zh: 我正在看的东西就是当前 focus（焦点）所在的内容。所以——而且我知道它现在是 focused（处于焦点中）的。然后当我和它说话时，它就知道要把那个 context（上下文）附到我正在看的那个东西旁边。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Okay, that makes sense.
And you're actually speaking, right?
This is like you're using monologue or

paragraphs.zh: 好的，这说得通。那你实际上是在用语音，对吧？这有点像你在用 monologue 还是——

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: whisper Yeah, or yeah.
Something like that, That's sort of my view of work and how things are going.
And we've been talking about, Okay, there's this the prize is there's a new way of working.
How does that fit into or differ from how you've been thinking about it and your strategy for granola?

paragraphs.zh: whisper，对，或者——对，差不多那类东西。这算是我对工作以及事情进展方式的看法。然后我们一直在讨论，Okay，所谓的 prize（目标/奖赏）是会出现一种新的工作方式。这和你一直以来对这件事的思考，以及你为 granola 制定的策略，是怎么契合的，或者说有什么不同？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I don't know.
I took this class once and they were talking about the difference between complex and con complicated problems.
Have have you heard this?
Yeah.
Yeah.

paragraphs.zh: 我也不确定。我之前上过一门课，里面他们讲过 complex 和 complicated 问题之间的区别。你听过这个说法吗？听过吧？对，对。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And it's, like, complicated.
It doesn't mean it's it's gonna be super hard, but it's, like, it's kinda knowable.
You know?
It's, like, you you and versus complex problems.
It's unknowable.

paragraphs.zh: complicated 的意思是，它不一定会超级难，但它是那种——某种程度上是可知的。你懂吧？就是——而和 complex 问题相比，后者是不可知的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: You have to, like, probe the system and see how it reacts and go from there.
So I think we're very much in a complex problem space here.
I don't have an overarching theory of where this is all going.
I think I could make one up that would sound plausible or as plausible as the next person's.
But I'm more in the mode of like, insights what or nuggets?

paragraphs.zh: 你必须去试探这个系统，看看它会如何反应，然后再从那里继续往下走。所以我觉得，我们现在非常明显就在一个 complex problem（复杂问题）的空间里。我并没有一个统摄全局的理论，来解释这一切最终会走向哪里。我觉得如果要编一个，我也能编出一个听起来还算合理的理论，或者说，和别人编出来的一样合理。但我现在更像是处在一种——寻找 insights（洞见），或者说 nuggets（零碎但有价值的信息/想法）的模式里。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And I'm like, oh, okay, I now this is like a thing that I've learned and now I believe to be true and I'm going to carry that with me.
So I have a few of those.
One is, seems like one fundamental design challenge in this agentic world is the time traveling problem.

Basically agents take time to do things and therefore, like when you you're talking about Slack as an interface for this, which is like the async delegation problem, I think you said before, which is basically like the moment in which you kick off a task and the moment in which you're reviewing the task are disjoint, and then you need to get all that context into your head.
And I think that'll be one of the I think you'll see a lot of the interfaces that evolve to be highly optimized for that.

paragraphs.zh: 我当时的感觉是，哦，好的，这是我现在学到的一件事，而且我相信它是对的，我会把它一直带在身上。所以我有几个这样的体会。其中一个是，在这个 agentic world（agent 驱动的世界）里，一个根本性的设计挑战似乎是 time traveling problem。基本上，agent 做事情需要时间，因此，比如你把 Slack 当作这个东西的界面时，这就像是你之前说的 async delegation problem（异步委派问题）：也就是说，你发起一个任务的那个时刻，和你回头审阅这个任务的那个时刻，是彼此分离的，然后你需要把所有这些上下文重新装进脑子里。我觉得这会是一个——我认为你会看到很多不断演化出来的界面，都会高度针对这个问题做优化。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And I haven't seen anything that's great, to be honest.
I don't know.
Conductor, it's like, oh, I've got all these different chat threads and I'm jumping between them when it's kind of ready.
Feels like they'll be I don't know what they look like.
There'll be evolutions beyond that that are coming.

paragraphs.zh: 老实说，我还没见过什么做得特别好的东西。我也不知道。Conductor 这种方式是，哦，我有这么多不同的聊天线程，然后我在它们之间来回切换，等它差不多准备好了再看。感觉未来还会有进一步的演化，我不知道它们具体会长什么样，但应该会超越现在这种形态。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Another one that's been that's interesting, like another way to get around this, like it takes a while for an agent to do thing problem is pre process a bunch of stuff.

Like if like a certain granola, like one thing that we figured out is like if I ask Granola to do something and I have to wait twenty seconds, basically humans will rarely wait for twenty seconds if they're in the back to back meetings chaotic crazy workday, which is kind of the user that we're thinking about.

But if Granola kind of thinks about something you might want and then pre generates it, and then at some point might be like, Oh, here, in case you want this, it's already here and all you have to do is click on it.
That's like feels completely different.
And so, like, we have we have some examples there.

paragraphs.zh: 另一个也很有意思的点是，针对“agent 做事要花一段时间”这个问题，还有一种绕开的办法，就是预处理一堆东西。拿 Granola 来说，我们发现的一件事是，如果我让 Granola 去做一件事，而我得等二十秒，基本上在那种一个接一个会议、混乱又疯狂的工作日里，人很少会愿意等二十秒，而这正是我们在设想的用户。但如果 Granola 能先想一想你可能会想要什么，然后提前把它生成好，接着在某个时刻对你说，哦，这个给你，以防你想要，它已经在这里了，你只需要点一下就行——那种感觉就完全不一样了。所以，我们在这方面已经有一些例子。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Like, we launched this thing recently where Granola will try to generate a brief for you before a meeting where it's basically, here's this person you're meeting with.
Here's the context of, like like, who the person is, if it's a first meeting or what you guys talked about last time, it's another meeting.

This one, you essentially have to pre generate it because it's useful when you're running two minutes late to a meeting and you're like, wait, who the heck is this person I'm talking And to that's the critical moment.
So you really only have a fifteen second window where it needs to be there or it's kind of useless.

So we actually pre generate millions of We pre generate a silly amount of these for a small percentage of them actually being opened in the belief that when you do open it though, you really, really appreciate it because it's exactly what you need in the moment of need.

paragraphs.zh: 比如，我们最近上线了一个功能：Granola 会在会议前尝试先为你生成一份 brief（简报）。基本上就是：这是你要见的人；这是相关背景，比如这个人是谁；如果是第一次见面，就给你第一次见面的背景；如果不是第一次见面，就告诉你们上次聊了什么。这个功能本质上必须预先生成，因为它最有用的场景是：你开会迟到了两分钟，正一边赶过去一边想，等等，我到底要和谁说话来着——那才是关键时刻。所以它实际上只有十五秒的窗口期，必须已经在那里，否则基本上就没用了。因此，我们实际上会预先生成几百万份——数量多得有点夸张——哪怕最后真正被打开的只占很小一部分。我们的判断是，一旦你真的打开它，你会非常、非常感激，因为它恰好就是你在那个需要的瞬间最需要的东西。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: It's an interesting process or trade off there, especially from a cost perspective because it's like hours and, I don't know, hundreds of thousands of hours of agents reasoning about things that may or may not ever see the light of day.

paragraphs.zh: 这是个很有意思的过程或者说权衡，尤其从成本角度看，因为这相当于花了成小时、我也不知道、几十万小时的 agent 推理时间，去思考一些最终可能永远都不会被看到的东西。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And how are you measuring that?
Whether that's worth it or not?

paragraphs.zh: 那你们怎么衡量这件事？怎么判断它到底值不值得？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: It's an open question.
I don't know how to measure it just yet.
What I'm trying to figure out is that we don't have a good metric between that.
Like there's use.
Like if something's not used at all, it's obviously not good.

paragraphs.zh: 这还是个开放问题。我现在还不知道该怎么衡量。我正在努力弄明白的是，我们现在缺少一个能很好描述这两者之间关系的指标。比如说，有“使用”这件事。如果一个东西完全没人用，那显然它就不好。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Right?

But then there's like, if something is used a little bit or a decent amount, but it's really load bearing when it's used and that's still very valuable, I'm trying to figure out how to measure what that is.

I was at a founder's conference the other day and four founders came up to me and talked about this pre meeting brief feature, which surprised me because it's not used as much as proportionally, that's not what I would have expected.
We have this analogy metaphor.
I always forget which of those two it is inside of granola of how we want the granola product to feel.

paragraphs.zh: 对吧？但另一种情况是，一个东西可能只被用了一点点，或者被用得还算可以，但它一旦被用到，就是非常 load bearing（承担关键负载、至关重要）的，那它依然非常有价值。我现在在想的是，怎么去衡量这种价值。前几天我去参加了一个 founder 的大会，有四位 founder 走过来跟我聊这个会前 brief 功能，这让我很惊讶，因为按使用占比来看，它并没有那么高，这并不是我原本会预期的。我们在 Granola 内部有一个 analogy / metaphor（类比 / 隐喻），我老是记不清这两个词到底该用哪一个，用来描述我们希望 granola 这个产品给人的感觉。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And it's like a handrail.
So if you imagine stairs, all stairs have handrails, right?
It's so good.
Basically you never notice a handrail.
It's invisible, but that moment you trip, your hand shoots out and it needs to be right there and it needs to be load bearing and it makes stairs way safer.

paragraphs.zh: 它就像一个扶手。你想象一下楼梯，所有楼梯都有扶手，对吧？这东西特别好。基本上你平时根本不会注意到扶手。它像是隐形的，但在你绊一下的那个瞬间，你的手会立刻伸出去，这时候它必须刚好在那里，而且还得能承重；它会让楼梯安全得多。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: That's how we think about granola, right?
We kind of want to get out of the way until you really need us.
But yeah, we probably need better metrics or frameworks to measure.
One thing we would thought is if we just take it away, if we take it away from a number of users, how much do they shout?
Or what kind of what's the impact of us doing that?

paragraphs.zh: 我们就是这样看 granola 的，对吧？我们有点希望在你真正需要我们之前，尽量不打扰你。不过，是的，我们可能还是需要更好的 metrics（指标）或 frameworks（框架）来衡量。有一件我们想过的事是：如果我们把它拿走——如果我们对一部分用户把它拿走——他们会有多大反应？或者说，我们这么做的影响到底是什么？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: But even that has it's not the best way.

paragraphs.zh: 但即便那样，也不是最好的办法。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Or maybe making like seeing if they will click to like generate click to generate as opposed to here it is, and it's already generated, but, you know, would you take the proactive action to do it?

paragraphs.zh: 或者也许可以这样：看看他们会不会去点那个 generate（生成）按钮，也就是“点击生成”，而不是直接把已经生成好的内容摆在这里；不过，你懂的，问题在于，他们会不会主动采取这个动作去生成它？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Of doing it and then it's there.
Yeah.
Yeah.
But then it's like, okay, they do, are we always going make them click?
I don't know.

paragraphs.zh: 就是说，他们自己去做，然后结果才会在那里。对，对。但接着问题又变成：好吧，他们确实会点，可我们难道要一直都让他们点击吗？我不知道。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: We could do it as a test, I guess.
A tricky one.

paragraphs.zh: 我想我们可以把这个当作一个测试来做。这个问题挺棘手的。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: That is tricky.
Then how do you think about also what is granola's role versus the codexes and the co works of the world?
And what do you see as being places where you want to integrate and make yourself available to what they do versus places where you're like, I want to own this.
This is like something that we can uniquely do well.

paragraphs.zh: 这确实很棘手。那你还会怎么去看 granola 的角色，以及它和这个世界上的 codexes、co works 之间的关系？你觉得哪些地方是你们想要做 integration（集成）、让自己能够接入他们所做事情的；又有哪些地方你会觉得，“这个我想自己掌控，这属于我们能独特地做好的东西”？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yeah.
So my view here is that it's like a new, again, it's like this new era of computing where for the first time computers can make sense of our context and you can do amazing things with that.
That end, having the right context is really critical in you being able to get value out of AI.
So my belief is that the context you have in granola, we should make that as available to however you want to use it.

So if you want to use it in Codecs, if you want to use it in your personal agent, if you want to use whatever you can do that if you can make your life better to use Granola context, should, and we should make that really easy.

paragraphs.zh: 对。所以我在这件事上的看法是，这像是一个新的——再说一次，这像是计算的新纪元：计算机第一次能够理解我们的 context（上下文），而你可以基于这一点做出非常惊人的事情。为此，拥有正确的 context，对于你能否从 AI 中获得价值是极其关键的。所以我的信念是，你在 granola 里拥有的 context，我们应该让它尽可能可用于任何你想使用它的方式。因此，如果你想在 Codecs 里用它，如果你想在你的 personal agent（个人 agent）里用它，或者不管你想怎么用，你都应该能这么做；如果使用 Granola 的 context 能让你的生活变得更好，那就应该这样，而且我们应该让这件事变得非常容易。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And there's a lot, you know, our API or MCP is going to get a lot better over the next couple months because that's like a first class goal of ours.
I think there are a number of jobs to be done, pain points, use cases that we should just be 5x better than anybody else in the world at.
The obvious ones are all related to meetings.
It's just one of those things where Maybe you feel differently.
Maybe you're more AI pill than I am.

paragraphs.zh: 而且有很多，你知道的，我们的 API 或 MCP 在接下来几个月里会变得好很多，因为那是我们的一个 first-class goal（最高优先级目标）。我觉得有不少 jobs to be done、pain points（痛点）、use cases（使用场景），我们就应该做到比世界上任何其他人都好 5 倍。最明显的那些都和会议有关。这就是那种事情——也许你感觉不同，也许你比我更 AI pill（更坚定相信 AI）。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I think that the models are going to keep getting better, but I think that if Granolah just cares about a few things way more than anybody else and we optimize the hell out of those things, they'll just be a better experience, especially if they're tied to a UI that you have to use in vivo during a meeting, for example.
And so that's our current strategy is basically be best in world in anything that's meeting adjacent and then be the best way to capture context to power any agents you have.
I can give you an example.
There's like, I was talking a user and they're doing something really clever.
She worked in sales and they basically used, I think it was Claude to generate a microsite before they would talk to a potential client.

paragraphs.zh: 我认为模型会持续变得更强，但我也认为，如果 Granolah 只是在少数几件事上比任何人都更在乎，并且把这些事情往死里优化，那体验就是会更好，尤其是当这些事情还和一个你必须在会议进行中、in vivo（现场/实时）使用的 UI 绑定在一起时。所以我们当前的策略基本上就是：凡是 meeting-adjacent（会议相关/邻近）的事情，都要做到世界最佳；然后再成为捕获 context（上下文）以驱动你拥有的各种 agent 的最佳方式。我可以举个例子。我之前和一个用户聊过，她们做了一件非常聪明的事。她在 sales 工作，她们基本上会在和潜在客户交谈之前，用我记得是 Claude 去生成一个 microsite。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And the microsite was it was really impressive.
They had this template and then of what that microsite would look like.
And then given a new potential customer, they would the cloud would, like, style that template to look like the customer's website.
And then they would connect the granola mcp and just pull all the context from granola and like fill out all the data and the fields and the widgets based on that data.
And that's just the kind of thing that we would never try to be best of the world at and wouldn't make sense to, right?

paragraphs.zh: 那个 microsite 真的很令人印象深刻。她们先有一个模板，规定这个 microsite 应该长什么样。然后给定一个新的潜在客户，Claude 就会把这个模板调整成看起来像那个客户自己的网站。接着她们再接入 granola mcp，把 granola 里的所有 context 都拉出来，然后基于这些数据去填充所有的数据项、字段和 widgets。像这种事情，我们根本不会尝试做到世界第一，而且那样做也没什么意义，对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: But she would also be, in her case she was like writing a lot of emails in granola right after meetings because like follow-up emails in granola made a lot of sense for her and that's the kind of thing where it's like maybe we don't want to write every email.

We don't want to your email inbox, but there might be a type of email that you want to send right after a meeting that is extremely good on certain dimensions that we could be the best in the world at.
It makes sense to do both.
I don't think it's a one or other.
I was undecided about this two years ago when we started Granola.

paragraphs.zh: 但与此同时，对她来说，她也会在会议结束后立刻在 granola 里写很多邮件，因为对她而言，在 granola 里写 follow-up emails（跟进邮件）非常合理。而这类事情就属于：也许我们不想去写所有邮件，我们也不想做你的 email inbox，但可能有一种你会在会后立刻发送的邮件类型，在某些维度上可以做得极其出色，而我们完全可以把它做到世界第一。两者都做是合理的。我不认为这是非此即彼。两年前我们刚开始做 Granola 的时候，我对这一点还拿不定主意。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Now I have strong conviction, but it kind of took me a while to figure that out.

paragraphs.zh: 现在我有很强的 conviction（确信），但我确实花了一些时间才把这件事想明白。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: That's interesting.
Yeah, I think from a user perspective and I may not be your core user, and that's actually really interesting to me to learn that.

Because of my the thesis that we're building every round and one of my convictions is what builders do now, people who want to figure out these general purpose tools and build these workflows or whatever, regular people are going to do in a few years once they're more built in once it's built into the handrails of these products.
And so what I do is I just play around with stuff and try to figure out, oh, this is what I use Codecs and Slack agents and whatever for.
And eventually, that will make its way into maybe stuff that we build, but stuff that the labs build or stuff that other people build, and we get to see it first.

paragraphs.zh: 这很有意思。是的，我觉得从用户视角来看——而且我可能不是你们的核心用户——知道这一点其实让我很感兴趣。因为我们在做的 thesis（判断框架）之一，以及我的一个 conviction，就是现在 builders 在做的事，那些想弄明白这些通用工具、搭建这些 workflows（工作流）之类的人在做的事，几年之后普通人也会去做——等这些能力被更好地内建进产品、变成这些产品的 handrails（默认护栏/引导）之后。所以我现在做的事，就是到处试各种东西，试着搞明白：哦，这就是我会把 Codecs、Slack agents 以及其他类似工具用在什么地方。最终，这些东西会进入也许是我们自己做的产品里，或者 labs 做的东西里，或者别人做的东西里，而我们只是更早看到它们。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Can I ask you a question about that?

Because I think, is the theory that, so the stuff that you are trying to automate with AI or the interactions you're trying to build, do you think that other people will also be building their own audit?
AI for people will be building their version of those automations, or do you think those will be productized?
Productized.
Yeah, okay.

paragraphs.zh: 我能就这个问你一个问题吗？因为我在想，你们的理论是不是这样：你现在试图用 AI 自动化的那些事情，或者你试图构建的那些交互，你觉得其他人之后也会自己去搭建他们版本的这些 automations（自动化），还是你觉得这些东西最终会被 productized（产品化）？会产品化。好，明白了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: I have the same view.
You're the guinea pigs, right?
Yes, Basically, yeah.
And we have tons of guinea pigs using granola, right?

My view is basically we should just look at what are the most common and cutting edge ways in which people are using our API and MCP to do crazy stuff and then figure out which of those do we think we could be the best in the world at and a lot of users would benefit from and then build a really beautiful seamless product experience that might have taken I ran into one of the founders of Hugging Face the other day at a conference and he was like, man, I had built this super convoluted complicated pre meeting brief workflow in Claude Code and I'd been working on it for months and I just turned it off because yours was better.

paragraphs.zh: 我看法也一样。你们就是 guinea pigs（小白鼠/先行试验者），对吧？对，基本上是这样。而且我们这边也有大量 guinea pigs 在用 granola，对吧？我的看法基本上是：我们应该去看，人们现在最常见、也最前沿地用我们的 API 和 MCP 做疯狂事情的方式到底是什么；然后判断其中哪些是我们认为自己能够做到世界第一、而且会让很多用户受益的；再把它做成一个真正漂亮、无缝的产品体验。前几天我在一个 conference 上碰到 Hugging Face 的一位创始人，他说，哥们，我之前在 Claude Code 里搭了一个超级绕、超级复杂的会前简报 workflow，已经折腾了好几个月，但我后来直接把它关掉了，因为你们的更好。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: And to me that was like, that's exactly what I want.
You can't do

paragraphs.zh: 对我来说，我当时就觉得，这正是我想要的。你没法做

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: this That's for

paragraphs.zh: 这个。这是为了

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: exactly right.
But that's exactly what I want because we're just going to care more than any individual should ever care about any one workflow is kind

paragraphs.zh: 完全正确。但那也正是我想要的，因为我们就是会比任何一个个体本该对任何单一 workflow（工作流）投入的关注都更多，

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: of how I think about it.
I think that's exactly right.
Okay.
So we're definitely aligned there.
My current feeling is, In general, when I and this is again, this is just for me and and this may change, but when I see granola, like a like the UI come up, it's usually a mistake.

paragraphs.zh: 我大概是这么想的。我觉得这完全对。好。所以我们在这点上肯定是一致的。我目前的感觉是，一般来说，当我看到 granola，比如它的 UI（用户界面）弹出来时，通常就说明哪里出错了。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: You don't want to see it.
I don't want to see it.
I mean, it's fine.
The recording thing is fine.
In general, the way that I want to access it is going to be it pushing something into codex, which is my work surface, or codex pulling something out of it.

paragraphs.zh: 你不会想看到它。我也不想看到它。我的意思是，录音这个功能没问题。总体来说，我希望访问它的方式是：要么它把某些东西推送到 codex——那是我的工作界面——要么 codex 从里面拉取一些东西。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And I think this focus on meetings is so right.
And it's actually such a hard and deep problem to just get that, like the context of that right.
So for example, I mean, you know this better than I, but I'm just thinking about when you do a transcript and I say the name Chris, which Chris are we talking about, right?
And so I think of the job of software companies in an AI world.
AI is this really super flexible thing, but it's only flexible if it's given the right structure and the right context within which to be flexible.

paragraphs.zh: 而且我觉得，聚焦会议这个方向太对了。实际上，光是把这里面的上下文处理对，就是一个非常困难而且很深的问题。比如说，我是说，你肯定比我更懂这个，但我在想的是，当你做 transcript（转录稿）时，如果我说了 Chris 这个名字，我们说的是哪个 Chris，对吧？所以我会这样理解 AI 世界里软件公司的工作：AI 是一种极其灵活的东西，但只有在给它正确的结构和正确的上下文、让它在其中发挥灵活性时，它才真正灵活。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: So if if, you know, AI if we're using the human body as an analogy, AI is like the the ligaments and the muscles and then software has to be the bones.
And the bones sort of set like give it form and a structure, but then the the AI can kind of like work around that to to do anything.
And there's so much for even even, for example, again, in a meeting transcript, I might say, that's a good idea.
But the way I say that means says a lot about what I actually think that is not captured in just a transcript.
And I want granola to be I this automation that runs that turns all the Slacks and all the meetings that I was not in into cards that I just go through.

paragraphs.zh: 所以如果——你知道——如果用人体来打比方，AI 就像韧带和肌肉，而软件必须是骨骼。骨骼某种程度上是在设定——给它形态和结构，但 AI 则可以围绕这个框架运作，去做各种事情。这里面还有太多东西，甚至——还是拿会议 transcript（转录稿）举例——我可能会说，“那是个好主意。”但我说这句话的方式，其实传达了很多我真正怎么想的信息，而这些是单靠 transcript 并不能捕捉到的。我希望 granola 成为——我是说——这样一种自动化：它持续运行，把所有 Slack 消息和所有我没参加的会议，整理成一张张卡片，供我快速浏览。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And I can see things that happened in the company.
But the transcripts are, A, often wrong, and B, they capture none of the nuance of how and why things were said.
And I think if Granolah did that for me, it would be the most valuable thing in the world, and I wouldn't necessarily have to look at the product.
And maybe for a more normie user who doesn't want to be in Codecs all the time, although I do think that that's going to be more and more the case.
You still want to have the UI, but there's all this background context processing that I would look to you guys to do that I don't really want to do.

paragraphs.zh: 这样我就能看到公司里发生了什么。但 transcript（转录稿）一来经常不准确，二来完全捕捉不到人们是如何、又为什么那样说话的细微差别。我认为如果 Granolah 能为我做到这一点，那它会是世界上最有价值的东西，而我甚至不一定需要去看这个产品本身。也许对于更普通的用户——他们不想一直待在 Codecs 里，尽管我确实觉得以后这种情况会越来越普遍——你还是需要保留 UI，但还有大量这种后台的上下文处理工作，我会希望你们来做，而不是我自己去做。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: Yeah.
